overview: The leadership and governance domain defines how higher education institutions establish strategic direction, oversight, and accountability for digitalisation and the adoption of artificial intelligence. It encompasses decision-making bodies, allocation of resources, and regulatory alignment at the institutional level. Strong governance ensures that digital transformation is not fragmented but guided by a common vision, balancing innovation with ethical, legal, and quality considerations. Currently, most higher education institutions lack formalised AI governance structures. Leadership practices in this area are often ad hoc, with responsibilities spread across multiple units without clear accountability. While some universities have begun integrating AI into their broader digital strategies, many are still at an early stage, relying on project-based initiatives rather than institution-wide frameworks. This fragmented approach results in uneven implementation, limited scalability, and challenges in ensuring transparency and trust among students, staff, and external stakeholders. Institutions that succeed in embedding AI into their governance models demonstrate greater coherence across faculties and more sustainable progress over time. Governance also provides transparency, ensuring that stakeholders can trust how AI and digitalisation are embedded into academic practice.

instructions.maturity_scoring: For each item/statement in the following table, select: [0] Not in place | [1] Partially in place | [2] Fully in place providing also clear evidence for the suggested score and owner responsible for this item. Examples for evidence and owner are provided within the table below. Note that items which are marked as Critical need to be implemented before approaching to higher maturity level (necessary/minimum conditions).

items.item: Digitalisation and AI explicitly integrated into institutional strategy

score_anchors.0: No approved strategy that names AI; drafts only or scattered mentions.
score_anchors.1: Approved strategy names AI, but the objectives or owners are vague or only cover parts of the HEI.
score_anchors.2: Approved strategy with AI objectives, principles, owners, and timelines that apply across the HEI.

items.evidence_examples: Board/Senate-approved strategy dated in the last 12 months; section on AI; named owners

items.item: Existence of a formal university-level body responsible for AI governance

score_anchors.0: No university- level body or only ad hoc groups.
score_anchors.1: Body exists on paper, or meets irregularly, or lacks terms of reference.
score_anchors.2: Body has terms of reference, named members, meeting schedule, minutes, and decisions tracked.

items.evidence_examples: ToR, membership list, meeting calendar, recent sets of minutes with actions

items.item: Regular alignment of institutional strategy with national/EU frameworks

score_anchors.0: No defined owner or schedule to track EU/national changes.
score_anchors.1: Owner named and reviews occur, but updates are ad hoc or not acted on.
score_anchors.2: Owner, schedule, and documented updates with changes reflected in policy or process.

items.evidence_examples: Policy record, note of AI Act readiness actions in the last 12 months

items.item: Clear allocation of recurrent budget for AI and digitalisation initiatives

score_anchors.0: One-off purchases or projects only; no recurrent budget line.
score_anchors.1: A recurrent budget exists but is limited in scope or unstable year to year.
score_anchors.2: Multi-year recurrent budget lines with cost centres and owners across the HEI.

items.item: Representation of staff and students in governance committees

score_anchors.1: Representatives named but with irregular participation or unclear role.
score_anchors.2: Representatives seated with a clear role, regular participation, and a feedback loop.

items.evidence_examples: Membership lists, minutes showing input, summaries of consultation

items.item: Monitoring and reporting mechanisms for progress across faculties

score_anchors.0: No KPI set; no central reporting.
score_anchors.1: KPIs defined and some faculties report, but coverage or cadence is partial.
score_anchors.2: KPI set, data owners named, HEI- wide reporting on a fixed cadence with a basic dashboard.

items.evidence_examples: KPI list, reporting calendar, latest annual or quarterly report, dashboard link
items.owners: Head of Institutional Research and Planning

items.item: Integration of AI and digitalisation into accreditation and quality processes

score_anchors.0: QA processes do not mention AI.
score_anchors.1: QA policy updated, but use is limited to pilots or selected programmes.
score_anchors.2: QA and accreditation templates include AI checks and are used in routine reviews HEI-wide.

items.evidence_examples: QA policy clause, revised review templates, two recent review reports using the clause

score_ranges.interpretation: Initial awareness of AI potential. Activities are fragmented, project-based, and lack formal governance, stable funding, and consistent policies. Decision- making is ad hoc.
Foundational structures are in place. AI appears in approved strategies, with emerging governance bodies, budgets, and reporting systems, though still uneven across faculties.

AI is fully embedded in institutional governance and quality systems. Processes are transparent, regularly reviewed, and aligned with EU and national frameworks, demonstrating leadership and accountability.

build_action_plan.intro: Before approaching a higher level of maturity in the leadership and governance AI DTP area, the critical items from 3.1.2 need to be fully implemented. For each unmet critical item, the necessary action needs to be specified to be performed (see table below).

foundational_actions.action: Appoint an executive sponsor and approve an AI governance mandate. Include decision rights and escalation path.
Publish a minimal AI use and disclosure policy. Specify permitted use, disclosure rules to students and staff, and prohibited data entry.
foundational_actions.owners: Legal/Policy Officer and Data Protection Officer (DPO)

foundational_actions.action: Create and approve a multi-year recurrent AI budget line with a named owner and cost centre.
Launch central monitoring and reporting. Define KPI set, assign data owners, set reporting cadence, and publish a basic dashboard.
foundational_actions.owners: Head of Institutional Research and Planning

foundational_actions.action: Update QA and accreditation templates to include AI checks and use them in live reviews.
Create an AI risk register and an incident response process with roles and timelines.
foundational_actions.owners: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) and Data Protection Officer (DPO)

foundational_actions.action: Formalise the university-level AI governance body with terms of reference, chair, members, and an annual meeting calendar; publish minutes.

Emerging.action: Map existing strategic documents and identify where digitalisation and AI are missing or inconsistent.
Establish a university-wide working group with representatives from the faculties and administration.
Initiate discussions with national agencies to align first steps with regulatory requirements.
Provide leadership with briefing papers or workshops on risks and opportunities of AI governance.

Established.action: Develop and publish a dedicated institutional strategy for AI and digitalisation, with measurable objectives. students and staff representatives in governance committees.
Create reporting mechanisms for faculties to submit annual updates on AI-related activities.
Established.owners: Head of Institutional Research and Planning

Established.action: Formalise stakeholder participation by including students and staff representatives in governance committees.
Allocate recurrent budget lines for digitalisation initiatives and link them to strategic goals.
Ensure compliance with quality and ethics requirements through integration into review processes.

Enhanced.action: Regularly update governance frameworks in line with national and EU policy (e.g., AI Act, Digital Decade).
Embed AI and digitalisation goals into institutional accreditation, quality assurance and long-term budgeting.
Enhanced.owners: Head of Quality Assurance and Finance Director

Enhanced.action: Establish transparent dashboards or annual reports for students, staff and external stakeholders.
Enhanced.owners: Head of Institutional Research and Planning

Enhanced.action: Share governance models, policies and lessons learned with other institutions through networks and conferences.
Create leadership development programmes to train deans, heads of department and senior staff in AI governance.

kpis.intro: The following table presents key performance indicators (KPIs) for this area and target values required to achieve each maturity level. For each KPI, the corresponding owner should be specified.

items.name: Percentage of faculties with aligned AI and digitalisation plans
Number of governance meetings per year addressing AI and digitalisation
items.owners: AI Governance Committee Chair and Secretariat

items.name: Percentage of initiatives with recurrent funding allocation
Percentage of programme / unit quality reviews that include AI and digitalisation elements in their criteria or templates (per QA cycle)
Student and staff satisfaction ratio with transparency of governance (survey)
items.owners: Communications Office and AI Gov. Secretariat

items.name: Number of policy updates to reflect national or EU regulatory changes

targets.Emerging: Formal review process defined; at least one AI/digitalisati on-related policy reviewed in last 3 years
targets.Established: Regular documented reviews (at least every 2–3 years) with at least one substantive update in response to national/EU changes
targets.Enhanced: All core AI/digitalisati on policies on a scheduled review cycle (≥every 2 years)

items.name: Percentage of AI projects monitored through central reporting mechanisms
items.owners: Head of Institutional Research and Planning

items.name: Training participation percentage of academic leaders involved in AI and digital governance
Percentage of stakeholder representation (staff and students) in governance bodies
Evidence of institutional benchmarking against peer universities in AI and digital governance

owners_and_roles.AI Governance Committee Chair: Multidisciplinary committee ensuring ethical, transparent, and compliant AI deployment.
owners_and_roles.AI Governance Committee Secretariat: Secretariat for AI governance and its committee
owners_and_roles.Chief Information Security Officer (CISO: Senior officer responsible for cybersecurity and AI-related risk management.
owners_and_roles.Data Protection Officer (DPO): Institutional authority for GDPR and privacy compliance.
owners_and_roles.Deans: Faculty-level leadership bodies managing academic and strategic implementation.
owners_and_roles.Faculty Councils: Faculty-level leadership bodies managing academic and strategic implementation.
owners_and_roles.Head of Institutional Research and Plann: Office responsible for institutional KPIs, analytics, and performance reporting.
owners_and_roles.Head of Institutional Quality: Manager responsible for QA and accreditation systems.
owners_and_roles.Head of Quality Assurance: Manager responsible for QA and accreditation systems.
owners_and_roles.Policy Officer: Unit for strategic planning and policy coordination.
